title: Take a chance on me
gist: A Resolution Foundation briefing note finds UK youth NEET numbers breached 1 million in Q1 2026 for the first time in 13 years, and argues that while broad cross-cohort hiring reforms create the most jobs, targeted schemes deliver far better fiscal value per additional job created.

insights.claim: The number of UK 16-24-year-olds not in education, employment or training (NEET) breached 1 million in the first quarter of 2026, the first time it has done so in thirteen years.
insights.speaker: Nye Cominetti
insights.so_what: A 13-year high in UK youth disengagement signals weakening labour-market absorption and rising fiscal/social cost pressure; watch UK consumer, education, and welfare-exposed names plus gilt issuance trajectory.
insights.quote: For the first time in thirteen years, the number of 16-24-year-olds in the UK not in education, employment or training (NEET) breached the 1 million mark.
insights.locator: The UK hit an unenviable milestone...

insights.claim: Even short spells of unemployment when young produce lasting 'scarring effects' on a person's employment, earnings and well-being across their entire working life.
insights.speaker: Nye Cominetti
insights.so_what: Scarring implies the 1m NEET cohort is a multi-decade drag on UK labour supply and productivity, not a transient cyclical blip — structurally bearish for UK potential growth.
insights.quote: even short periods of unemployment when young can have ‘scarring effects’ on employment, earnings and well-being throughout one’s working life

themes.theme: Policy design: targeted vs broad reforms

insights.claim: Resolution Foundation concludes that the most important choice in youth-employment policy is between reforms affecting all young people versus targeted schemes, outranking all other design considerations.
insights.speaker: Nye Cominetti
insights.so_what: Frames how UK fiscal support for youth hiring is likely to be structured; signals policymakers may favour narrow, cheaper schemes over universal wage subsidies.
insights.quote: none is more important than deciding between reforms that affect all young people versus targeted schemes
insights.locator: Policy interventions that aim to boost firms’ appetite...

insights.claim: Broad cross-cohort policy reforms create the largest absolute number of extra youth jobs, but targeted schemes deliver much better value for money on fiscal cost per additional job created.
insights.speaker: Nye Cominetti
insights.so_what: Cost-per-job efficiency argument points to targeted subsidies as the likely Treasury preference in a constrained fiscal environment — relevant for sectors that would receive hiring incentives.
insights.quote: although cross-cohort policy reforms have the biggest effect in terms of extra jobs created, targeted schemes offer much better value for money when we consider the fiscal cost per additional job created

insights.claim: The briefing note estimates the gross Exchequer cost of each additional full-time job created by a range of policy reforms to compare their impact and cost-effectiveness.
insights.speaker: Nye Cominetti
insights.so_what: A standardized cost-per-job metric is the lens through which forthcoming UK youth-employment policy will be judged; useful for anticipating which interventions survive fiscal scrutiny.
insights.quote: We estimate the gross cost to the Exchequer of each additional full-time job created by a range of policy reforms
